5710.description: sshd: Attempt to login using a non-existent user
5710.meaning: Someone tried to SSH in with a username that does not exist on the host. Common in internet-facing brute-force scans.

5716.meaning: SSH password or key authentication failed for an existing (or attempted) account.

5715.meaning: Successful SSH login. Correlate with prior failures from the same source when investigating brute force.

5503.meaning: PAM-reported failed login on Linux (console, sudo, or service auth depending on context).

31103.description: SQL injection attempt detected in web request
31103.meaning: Web request matched SQL-injection patterns. Review the URL/body and whether the app blocked or executed it.

533.meaning: The set of listening ports on the agent changed since the last check (new service or unexpected listener).

100100.description: Multiple failed logins followed by success (possible brute force)
100100.meaning: Composite signal: several authentication failures then a success — classic brute-force success pattern. Investigate immediately.

60122.description: Logon Failure - Unknown user or bad password
60122.meaning: Windows Event 4625-style failed logon (unknown user or bad password).

authentication_failed.meaning: Single failed authentication events (Linux/SSH and related). Use for failed-login counts and triage.

authentication_failures.meaning: Multi-event / correlation rules for repeated auth failures (often brute-force composites).

win_authentication_failed.meaning: Windows failed logon events (Event ID 4625 family).

authentication_success.meaning: Successful authentication events (e.g. SSH accept). Pair with failures when hunting brute force.

sshd.meaning: Parses OpenSSH daemon log lines into fields such as srcip, dstuser, and status.

web-accesslog.meaning: Parses common HTTP access-log formats for web attack rules.

windows_eventchannel.meaning: Parses Windows Event Channel security events (including failed logons).
